responses.response:
This argument claims that S4 (compensation without structural change) entrenches a two-tier system while appearing to fix it, and that visible success in funded general schools relieves political pressure for the structural reform that equity advocates believe is necessary. We take this seriously because it identifies a real dynamic: if S4 demonstrably narrows the SES-outcome gap, policymakers may declare the problem solved and defund the compensation. However, the argument contains two vulnerabilities we must address directly.

First, the claim assumes that 'compensation and structural preservation are politically substitutable'—i.e., that funding general schools is a substitute for abolishing selective schools, and that success at substitution is a problem. But this assumption begs the question of whether structural change (abolishing selective schools) is actually necessary to achieve equity. A10 asserts that compensation 'entrenches a two-tier system while appearing to fix it,' but this presupposes that a two-tier system cannot deliver equity without structural abolition. That is precisely what is contested. If S4 succeeds in narrowing the SES-outcome gradient in general schools *without* eliminating selective schools, then the two-tier system has been reformed, not entrenched. The 'appearance of fixing' is only a problem if the underlying diagnosis is correct—i.e., if selective schools are the *structural cause* of inequality that cannot be addressed downstream. We do not accept that diagnosis.

Second, A10 conflates two different failure modes: (a) S4 fails to produce equity gains, in which case it deserves to be abandoned; (b) S4 succeeds but the success is used to justify not pursuing S2 or S3. The argument is really about (b)—the political risk that success becomes an excuse for inaction on structural reform. But this is a claim about what equity advocates will demand next, not a claim that S4 is incoherent or unjust. If S4 produces measurable improvements in general-school outcomes and SES-outcome gaps narrow, and if non-state schools continue to serve families who value selective education, then both equity and plurality have been advanced. The fact that equity advocates may then demand *more* reform does not invalidate the achievements of S4.

That said, the argument does point to a real political vulnerability in our position: we are relying on S4 to deliver equity gains that satisfy equity advocates enough that the pressure to abolish selective schools subsides. This is a bet on political durability that may not hold. If the Ministry funds S4 inadequately, or if general-school outcomes improve only marginally, then A10's concern—that the system appears to be fixed while remaining fundamentally unequal—becomes justified, and we would lose credibility.

We therefore acknowledge the condition that would shift our evaluation: if S4 is implemented but produces negligible equity gains (e.g., the SES-outcome gap narrows by less than 5 percentage points over five years, or general-school PISA scores improve but do not close the gap with selective-school cohorts), then the compensation strategy has failed, and the structural question returns with greater force. In that case, opposition to S3 becomes harder to sustain, because we would have no credible alternative reform path to offer. We would need to revisit S2 (phased caps) or propose a modified S3 (comprehensive schools with explicit mission exemptions for non-state schools).
responses.new_condition: If S4 is implemented at adequate funding levels (≥ 7% real budget increase for TEIP-equivalent schools) and produces demonstrable equity gains within five years (narrowing of the SES-outcome gap by ≥ 5 percentage points, or convergence of general-school PISA to within 15 points of selective-school cohorts), support is strengthened and opposition to S2/S3 remains firm. If S4 produces negligible gains after five years of full funding, conditional support for S4 becomes qualified, and we acknowledge that the case for structural reform (S2 or a modified S3 with mission exemptions) becomes stronger, though opposition to S3 as currently framed does not convert to support.

responses.response:
This argument claims that Finland's equity gains came from a bundle (structure + teacher policy + support), not from structure alone, and that adopting only the structural component (S3) wastes the reform and damages trust in reform generally. This is a serious objection because it directly undermines the causal logic of S3: if the success of comprehensive schooling in Finland depended on simultaneously upgrading teacher quality, diagnostic support, and curriculum, then S3—which proposes structural abolition without simultaneous investment in the full bundle—is a high-risk partial implementation.

We agree with the premise. The scenario itself acknowledges that 'Hungary currently lacks' the teacher quality and diagnostic support that made the Finnish transition work, and that 'comprehensive schools can be equipped with the teacher quality' only over time. This is an admission that S3 is proposing the structural change first and the enabling conditions second. A8 correctly identifies this as a risk: if the structure is abolished before the bundle is complete, classrooms will face comprehensive organization without the teacher training and support that make comprehensive teaching work. The damage to trust is real—policymakers will have claimed that S3 is necessary for equity, and when results disappoint (because the bundle was incomplete), equity advocates and teachers will have lost confidence in reform more broadly.

However, A8 does not fully address why this risk supports our position rather than modifying it. The argument could be understood as saying: 'S3 is a good policy, but it requires simultaneous implementation of the full bundle, which is hard.' If that is the claim, then it is an argument for *delaying* S3 until the teacher-policy and diagnostic infrastructure are ready—not an argument against S3 itself. Under that reading, we agree: S3 should not proceed until the bundle can be implemented together.

But A8 seems to go further: it is raised by 'konzervativ_ertekvedo' and 'kisgyerekes_szuloi' (conservative value-defenders and parents seeking choice), which suggests the claim is being used to argue not just for a delay, but against S3 as a policy choice at all. If that is the intended interpretation, then A8 becomes an argument that the bundling requirement is so stringent that S3 is effectively infeasible—and therefore other paths (S1, S2, S4) should be preferred.

We accept the bundling claim as factually grounded (A8 is labeled as 'fact' and the decomposability assumption is 'contested'). We do not accept that the bundling requirement defeats S3 categorically. Rather, we propose that S3 should be reframed as a *sequenced* reform: (1) Years 1–7: invest heavily in teacher training, diagnostic capacity, curriculum redesign, and pilot comprehensive models in willing non-state schools (with exemptions). (2) Years 7–10: if teacher supply and diagnostic infrastructure reach adequate levels, legislate the abolition of selective tracks. (3) If, after 7 years, the infrastructure is insufficient, the sequence is paused and alternative paths (S2 or enhanced S4) are pursued.

This addresses A8's concern without abandoning S3 entirely. It also respects our core position: non-state schools should not be forced into comprehensive structures before the infrastructure exists to support them. If the Minister commits to a sequenced approach and guarantees that non-state schools can continue selective tracks during the 7-year build-up, opposition to S3 becomes qualified support for the sequence, not blanket opposition to the final state (comprehensive schooling).
responses.new_condition: We revise our stance on S3 from blanket opposition to conditional opposition if the reform is resequenced: (1) teacher training, diagnostic support, and curriculum materials are built first (Years 1–7) with non-state schools exempted or invited to pilot voluntarily; (2) abolition of selective tracks is legislated only after infrastructure reaches defined readiness thresholds (e.g., 80% of teachers trained in comprehensive pedagogy, diagnostic capacity established in all districts, curriculum materials field-tested); (3) non-state schools retain the right to continue selective operations during the build-up, and explicit legal guarantees protect mission-driven admissions (denominational, linguistic) if/when they transition to comprehensive structures. If the Ministry proposes such a sequenced approach with credible timelines and measurable infrastructure targets, opposition becomes conditional support for the sequence, contingent on meeting the readiness thresholds before legislative abolition of selective tracks. If S3 is legislated without sequencing (i.e., structure changed first, bundle built after), opposition remains firm.
